sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image after RIGHT:
- Player is at the left.
- Green (N) is connected to the player (there's a gray connection line).
- Red (R) is disconnected.
- Orange (O) is above R (disconnected).
- Blue (b) is below N (disconnected).
The board looks the same as before. Let me check the positions.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check positions after RIGHT
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Board blocks:")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    b = node['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    if min(rows) < 50 and node['color'] in ['N', 'b', 'O', 'R', 'M']:
        if node['color'] == 'M' and node['pixels'] == 18:
            print(f"  Player: rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)} cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
        elif node['color'] == 'M' and node['pixels'] == 4:
            pass
        else:
            print(f"  {node['color']}: rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)} cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Check last action result
print("\nLast action result:", last_action_result)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:
After RIGHT:
- N: rows 25-28, cols 18-21 (shifted from 12-15 to 18-21, +6 cols)
- Player: rows 24-29, cols 5-10 (unchanged)
So RIGHT shifted N right by 6 cols. N is still connected to the player.
So the pattern is:
- LEFT shifts connected blocks left (and disconnects the rightmost one if there are multiple).
- RIGHT shifts connected blocks right (and disconnects the leftmost one if there are multiple? Or does it not disconnect?).
Wait, earlier when I had Player - N - O connected and moved RIGHT, N shifted right and O stayed. So RIGHT disconnected O (the rightmost block). But now I have Player - N connected, and RIGHT shifted N right without disconnecting it.
Hmm, so the pattern is:
- LEFT: shifts connected blocks left, disconnects the rightmost block.
- RIGHT: shifts connected blocks right, disconnects the rightmost block.
Wait, that doesn't make sense. Let me re-examine.
Earlier, I had Player - N - O connected. After RIGHT, N shifted right and O stayed. So O (the rightmost block) disconnected.
Now, I have Player - N connected. After RIGHT, N shifted right. N didn't disconnect because it's the only connected block (and also the rightmost one).
Wait, but if RIGHT disconnects the rightmost block, then N should have disconnected. But N didn't disconnect.
Hmm, maybe the rule is different. Let me think about this more carefully.
Actually, looking at the earlier case: Player - N - O connected. After RIGHT, N shifted right and O stayed. So the chain became Player - N, and O disconnected.
But now, Player - N connected. After RIGHT, N shifted right. The chain is still Player - N.
So maybe RIGHT doesn't disconnect the rightmost block. Maybe RIGHT shifts all connected blocks right, and the rightmost block disconnects only if it was already disconnected (i.e., at the end of the chain).
Wait, that doesn't make sense either.
Let me re-examine the earlier case. When I had Player - N - O connected and moved RIGHT:
- N shifted from cols 18-21 to 24-27 (+6).
- O stayed at cols 30-33.
- The chain became Player - N, and O disconnected.
So RIGHT caused O (the rightmost block) to disconnect. But N (the middle block) shifted right.
Now, when I have Player - N connected and move RIGHT:
- N shifted from cols 12-15 to 18-21 (+6).
- The chain is still Player - N.
So RIGHT caused N to shift right, but N didn't disconnect.
The difference is that in the first case, there were 2 connected blocks (N and O), and the rightmost one (O) disconnected. In the second case, there was 1 connected block (N), and it didn't disconnect.
So maybe the rule is: RIGHT shifts all connected blocks right, and the rightmost block disconnects only if there are multiple connected blocks.
Hmm, but that's a weird rule. Let me think about this differently.
Actually, maybe the rule is: RIGHT shifts all connected blocks right, and the rightmost block disconnects if it was already at the end of the chain (i.e., not connected to anything on its right).
Wait, that's the same as saying the rightmost block always disconnects. But in the second case, N didn't disconnect.
OK, let me try a different hypothesis. Maybe the rule is:
- LEFT: shifts all connected blocks left by 6 cols. The rightmost block disconnects.
- RIGHT: shifts all connected blocks right by 6 cols. The rightmost block disconnects only if it's not the only connected block.
Actually, I think the rule is simpler. Let me re-examine.
When I have Player - N - O connected and move RIGHT:
- The player stays at cols 5-10.
- N shifts from 18-21 to 24-27 (+6).
- O stays at 30-33.
- The chain becomes Player - N, and O disconnects.
So N shifted right, and O (which was to the right of N) stayed. The gap between N and O increased from 3 cols to 6 cols (30 - 27 = 3, but after shift, 30 - 27 = 3... wait, 30 - 27 = 3, and 30 - 24 = 6).
Actually, before RIGHT: N at 18-21, O at 30-33. Gap = 30 - 21 = 9 cols.
After RIGHT: N at 24-27, O at 30-33. Gap = 30 - 27 = 3 cols.
So the gap decreased from 9 to 3. And O disconnected.
Hmm, maybe the rule is: RIGHT shifts connected blocks right, and if a connected block overlaps with a disconnected block, the disconnected block stays and the connected block disconnects.
Wait, that doesn't make sense either.
Let me try a different approach. Let me focus on what I need to do to solve the level.
The bottom panel shows R, O, b, N. The white border is on R (or maybe on N). I need to figure out what the goal is.
Actually, let me look at the bottom panel more carefully. In the image, the bottom panel shows:
- Pink square (player) with white border
- Red square
- Orange square
- Blue square
- Green square with white border
So the white border is on the player and on N (green). Maybe this indicates that N is the currently connected block.
But earlier, when I had N and O connected, the white border was on N and O. And when I had only N connected, the white border was on N.
So the white border indicates connected blocks. And the player always has a white border.
OK, so the bottom panel shows the order of blocks from left to right: R, O, b, N. And the white border indicates which blocks are connected.
Currently, only N is connected (white border on N). R, O, and b are disconnected (no white border).
So the goal might be to connect all blocks in the order R, O, b, N. But N is already connected, and it's the last block in the order.
Hmm, maybe the goal is to connect blocks in reverse order: N, b, O, R. And N is already connected.
Let me try to connect b to N. b is at rows 31-34, cols 18-21. N is at rows 25-28, cols 18-21. They're on the same column, but different rows.
To connect b to N, I need to move DOWN to b's row. But N is connected to the player, so moving DOWN will move N with the player.
If I move DOWN, N will shift from rows 25-28 to rows 31-34. And b is at rows 31-34. So N and b will overlap.
Let me try DOWN to see what happens.
